expanded_description: Mastering rotations as rigid transformations around a fixed center, with emphasis on coordinate rules about the origin, systematic processes for arbitrary centers, and analyzing rotational symmetry

core_concept.principle: A rotation turns every point of a figure around a fixed center by a specified angle and direction, preserving distances, angle measures, and orientation
core_concept.explanation: Unlike reflections, rotations do not reverse the clockwise/counterclockwise order of vertices. By mathematical convention, positive angles rotate counterclockwise (CCW) and negative angles rotate clockwise (CW). Rotations about the origin follow elegant coordinate transformation patterns that depend only on the angle of rotation.

image.alt: Triangle rotated 90 degrees counterclockwise around the origin showing preserved side lengths and orientation
image.caption: Figure 1: Rotation preserves shape, size, and vertex order while changing position

sections.section_title: Coordinate Rules for Rotations About the Origin
sections.introduction: The origin $(0,0)$ is the standard center of rotation in coordinate geometry. Special angles yield simple, predictable coordinate transformations that can be memorized and applied directly.

key_concept.definition: For rotations centered at the origin, each point $(x,y)$ maps to $(x',y')$ according to specific angle-based rules that preserve distance from the origin
key_concept.context: These rules are derived from the unit circle and trigonometric relationships, but can be applied algebraically without advanced trigonometry

image.alt: Point (x,y) rotated through 90, 180, and 270 degrees showing coordinate changes
image.caption: Figure 2: Standard rotation rules about the origin for quadrant angles

characteristics: New x becomes negative of old y

image.alt: Point (4,2) rotated to (-2,4) showing 90 degree arc
image.caption: Figure 3: 90° CCW moves (x,y) to quadrant II or III depending on sign
Figure 4: 180° rotation is equivalent to point reflection through the origin

classifications.value: Three-quarter turn CCW or quarter turn CW
characteristics: New y becomes negative of old x

image.alt: Point (-2,-6) rotated to (-6,2) showing 270 degree arc
image.caption: Figure 5: 270° CCW = 90° CW, both yield (y,-x)

worked_examples.title: Rotating a Triangle 90° CCW About the Origin
worked_examples.problem: Triangle $ABC$ has vertices $A(3,1)$, $B(5,4)$, and $C(1,5)$. Rotate $\triangle ABC$ 90° counterclockwise about the origin. Find the coordinates of $\triangle A'B'C'$ and verify side $AB$ length is preserved.
worked_examples.concept_applied: 90° CCW coordinate rule and rigid motion property

image.alt: Original and rotated triangles with circular rotation arcs
image.caption: Figure 6: All vertices follow congruent 90° paths around the origin

sections.introduction: When rotating around a point other than the origin, or using non-standard angles, a structured geometric or algebraic approach ensures accuracy.

key_concept.definition: To rotate about an arbitrary center $(h,k)$, translate the system so the center moves to the origin, apply the rotation, then translate back
key_concept.context: This three-step method (translate-rotate-translate) works for any center and any angle, leveraging the simplicity of origin rotations

image.alt: Diagram showing shifting center to origin, rotating, then shifting back
image.caption: Figure 7: The universal method for rotations about any center point

components.interpretation: Subtract center coordinates to shift to origin, apply 2D rotation matrix, then add center coordinates back

image.alt: Rotation matrix applied to translated coordinates
image.caption: Figure 8: General rotation formula derived from trigonometric identities

process_flow.description: Note the rotation center $(h,k)$, angle $\theta$, and direction (positive=CCW, negative=CW)
process_flow.mechanism: Mark center point on grid; draw reference ray for angle measurement

process_flow.description: Subtract $h$ from all x-coordinates and $k$ from all y-coordinates
Use standard origin rules or rotation matrix on relative coordinates
Add $h$ to new x and $k$ to new y to return to original coordinate system
Plot image, confirm distances to center match preimage, check angle between corresponding segments equals $\theta$

image.alt: Verification showing equal radii and angle arcs
image.caption: Figure 9: Every preimage-image pair forms congruent isosceles triangles with the center

principles.statement: Rotations maintain the original clockwise/counterclockwise ordering of vertices; they are direct isometries
principles.application: Distinguish rotations from reflections, which reverse orientation
principles.example: If $A \to B \to C$ is counterclockwise, then $A' \to B' \to C'$ remains counterclockwise after any rotation
principles.analogy: Like turning a steering wheel—the hand positions move, but left stays left and right stays right

worked_examples.title: Rotating a Point 90° CW About (2,1)
worked_examples.problem: Point $P(5,4)$ is rotated 90° clockwise about the center $C(2,1)$. Find the coordinates of $P'$.

image.alt: Point P rotated around center C with translation steps shown
image.caption: Figure 10: Shifting reference frame simplifies non-origin rotations

sections.introduction: Many geometric and natural figures map perfectly onto themselves when rotated by certain angles less than 360°. This property is called rotational symmetry.

key_concept.definition: A figure has rotational symmetry if it coincides with itself after a rotation of $\theta < 360°$ about its center
key_concept.context: The smallest such angle is called the angle of symmetry, and the number of times it maps onto itself in one full turn is the order of symmetry $n$, where $\theta = \frac{360°}{n}$

image.alt: Regular pentagon showing 5 positions of self-coincidence during 360 rotation
image.caption: Figure 11: Order $n=5$, angle $= 72°$ for regular pentagon

categories.description: Figure only maps onto itself after a full 360° rotation
categories.detailed_mechanism: No angle $<360°$ produces a perfect match; common in scalene triangles, irregular polygons, and letters like F, G, P

examples.explanation: All sides and angles differ; no partial rotation aligns vertices

image.caption: Figure 12: Only 360° rotation returns to original position

categories.description: Figure maps onto itself after 180° rotation
categories.detailed_mechanism: Point symmetry about the center; each point has a matching opposite point at equal distance

image.caption: Figure 13: Parallelograms have rotational symmetry but no line symmetry

categories.description: Figure maps onto itself multiple times per full rotation
categories.detailed_mechanism: Regular n-gons have order $n$; angle $= 360°/n$. Higher orders indicate greater symmetry density

image.alt: Hexagon rotated in 60 degree increments showing self-coincidence
image.caption: Figure 14: Order 6, angle 60° for regular hexagon

examples.explanation: Infinite order; aligns at any rotation angle

image.caption: Figure 15: Circles possess continuous (infinite) rotational symmetry

principles.statement: For a figure with finite rotational symmetry, $\text{Angle} = \frac{360°}{\text{Order}}$ and $\text{Order} = \frac{360°}{\text{Smallest Angle}}$
principles.application: Quickly determine one value when given the other without testing multiple rotations
principles.example: A figure with 40° minimum rotation angle has order $360/40 = 9$. A star with order 8 aligns every $360/8 = 45°$
principles.analogy: Like slicing a pie—more equal slices means smaller slice angles but higher count

worked_examples.title: Analyzing Symmetry of a Composite Star Figure
worked_examples.problem: A regular five-pointed star has identical arms spaced evenly. Determine its order of rotational symmetry and smallest angle of rotation. If one point is at $(0,4)$ and center at $(0,0)$, find coordinates of the next point clockwise after rotation.

step_by_step.explanation: Regular 5-pointed star matches itself 5 times per full turn

step_by_step.step: Set up rotation for next point
step_by_step.explanation: Rotate $(0,4)$ by 72° CW (or 288° CCW) about origin

worked_examples.concept_applied: Rotational symmetry order/angle relationship and coordinate application

image.alt: Five-pointed star with rotation angles marked
image.caption: Figure 16: Regular stars exhibit order n rotational symmetry matching point count

summary: A rotation is a rigid transformation that turns a figure around a fixed center by a specified angle and direction while preserving distances, angle measures, and orientation. Positive angles rotate counterclockwise, negative angles clockwise. For rotations about the origin, standard quadrant angles follow simple coordinate rules: 90° CCW $(x,y) \to (-y,x)$, 180° $(x,y) \to (-x,-y)$, and 270° CCW/90° CW $(x,y) \to (y,-x)$. To rotate about an arbitrary center $(h,k)$, use the translate-rotate-translate method: shift coordinates by subtracting $(h,k)$, apply origin rules, then shift back by adding $(h,k)$. Rotational symmetry occurs when a figure maps onto itself after a rotation less than 360°. The order $n$ indicates how many times it aligns per full turn, with the smallest angle of rotation given by $360°/n$. Regular polygons and stars have order equal to their number of vertices, parallelograms have order 2 (point symmetry), and circles have infinite order. Mastering rotations enables precise geometric manipulation, computer graphics transformations, engineering design, and symmetry analysis in mathematics and nature.
